summary.category: Basics of Reproduction and DNA Copying
points: Reproduction is not essential for individual survival but is necessary for species continuity; it fundamentally involves creating a DNA copy along with a new cellular apparatus.
DNA copying mechanisms are not perfectly accurate, leading to subtle genetic variations that provide a survival advantage in changing environmental niches and serve as the basis for evolution.

Single-celled and simple multi-cellular organisms use asexual methods: fission (binary in Amoeba, multiple in Plasmodium), fragmentation (Spirogyra), regeneration (Planaria), budding (Hydra, Yeast), and spore formation (Rhizopus).
Many plants predictably reproduce through vegetative propagation, developing new, genetically similar plants from parts like roots, stems, or leaves (e.g., Potato, Bryophyllum).

summary.category: Sexual Reproduction in Plants and Humans
points: Sexual reproduction combines DNA from two individuals, utilizing meiosis to halve chromosomes in specialised germ-cells (gametes), which dramatically speeds up the generation of useful variations.
In flowering plants, pollen transfers from the stamen to the pistil (pollination) enabling fertilisation in the ovary and eventual seed germination.

Human reproduction involves sexual maturation at puberty (indicated by bodily changes and menstruation) and specific reproductive systems; maintaining reproductive health through contraception prevents sexually transmitted diseases and unwanted pregnancies.
